blocks.statement: I can use conservation of momentum to compute the Δv DART gave to Dimorphos, and I can apply the same calculation to 2026-XJ to estimate what it would take to deflect it.

blocks.whatWeKnow: On Sept 26, 2022, NASA's DART spacecraft (~600 kg, ~6 km/s) crashed into a small asteroid called Dimorphos (~5 × 10⁹ kg). It changed Dimorphos's orbital period by 32 minutes.
blocks.connection: This is the day the unit pays off. Today's math is the math we'd use on 2026-XJ. If the Δv we need is DART-scale, deflection is on the table. If not, we'd need a much bigger push (or a different strategy).

blocks.markdown:
## DART, in two equations

**Equation 1 — the strike.** Treat the impact as perfectly inelastic with M ≫ m:

**Δv ≈ m·v / M** — spacecraft momentum, spread over the asteroid's mass.

**Equation 2 — why tiny works.**

**offset = Δv × lead time**

If Δv ≈ 1 mm/s and we have 1 year (3.15 × 10⁷ s): offset ≈ 31.5 km. The push is microscopic; the **time** does the heavy lifting. More lead time = more offset. Linear in both.

terms.definition: NASA's 2022 mission that deliberately crashed a 600-kg spacecraft into a small asteroid to test whether kinetic impact can change its trajectory. DART hit Dimorphos at ~6 km/s, delivering only ~3 mm/s of Δv — but that tiny push, accumulated over time, shifts position by kilometers.
terms.cognate: Sp. misión DART · Pt. missão DART · HC misyon DART

blocks.prompt: DART (m = 600 kg) hit Dimorphos (M = 5 × 10⁹ kg) at v = 6 × 10³ m/s, treating the impact as perfectly inelastic. Find the velocity change Δv of the asteroid. (Hint: M ≫ m, so (m+M) ≈ M.)
blocks.given: m = 600 kg · v = 6000 m/s · M = 5×10⁹ kg · asteroid initially at rest in DART's frame
blocks.equation: m·v + M·0 = (m + M)·Δv ≈ M·Δv → Δv ≈ m·v / M
blocks.work:
Δv ≈ (600 × 6000) / (5 × 10⁹)
Δv ≈ 3.6 × 10⁶ / 5 × 10⁹
Δv ≈ 7.2 × 10⁻⁴ m/s ≈ 0.72 mm/s
(NASA measured ≈ 2.7 mm/s with momentum enhancement from ejecta — same order of magnitude.)
blocks.answer: ≈ 0.72 mm/s (less than a millimeter per second). Tiny — but it adds up.

blocks.prompt: 2026-XJ has mass M ≈ 1 × 10⁹ kg and will pass Earth in 6 months (t ≈ 1.5 × 10⁷ s). We need to shift its position by R_Earth ≈ 6.4 × 10⁶ m to miss us. What Δv does it need? Then: would a DART-scale strike (600 kg at 6 km/s) deliver it?
blocks.givenHint: Required: Δv = shift / time. Available from one DART: Δv = m·v / M with M = 10⁹ kg.
blocks.equationHint: Compute the NEEDED Δv first, then the DELIVERED Δv, then compare.

blocks.title: Read your own gap
blocks.markdown: When you compare the required Δv to what a single DART gives: the gap tells you whether one impactor is enough, or whether we'd need many — or need to have launched **sooner**.

blocks.prompt: Complete the deflection frame, then answer: why doesn't NASA need a HUGE Δv to deflect an asteroid? Use 'lead time' and 'small change in velocity' in your answer.
blocks.frame: To deflect 2026-XJ by one Earth-radius in 6 months, we need Δv ≈ ___. A single DART-class strike would give about ___. So we'd need ___ DARTs (or more lead time).
